title: Is This The End Of The US’ Exorbitant Privilege? | April 12, 2025

Let's get into the uh executive summary
here. Uh like I said, I think this is
the most important around the horn that
uh we've done as a firm and may ever do
as a firm. So, uh grab your popcorn.
This week's key macro question is, is
this the beginning of the end of the
US's exorbitant privilege? And that's a
huge question. Uh I'm certainly not
smart enough to
determine the answer ex ante. I
certainly won't talk with any degree of
certainty here. Uh this is all
probabilistic. However, I do think we
need to ask and try to answer the
question, and we're going to do that for
you today uh as best as we can. Um so,
our answers are uh in our view, uh this
past week represents the most
transformational week in asset markets
since the Lehman Brothers bankruptcy
catalyzed nearly two decades of fiscal
and monetary policy largess.
Geopolitically driven stress in the
Treasury market is accelerating the
transition from US exceptionalism to the
US being an emerging market in capital
flight risk terms. President Trump may
be operating under the assumption that
the US is too big to fail, and foreign
creditors may check him on that. Markets
thought Lehman was too big to fail, too.
This transition has several profound
implications for asset markets. Number
one, the rest of the world has much more
negotiating leverage than anyone in the
Trump administration is willing to admit
publicly, increasing the probability
that reciprocal tariff rates are broadly
lowered in deals that President Trump
will celebrate as wins, but are likely
less effective than the phase one trying
to change trade deal and USMCA he
negotiated during his first term. We
wouldn't be here today if the art of the
deal actually worked during his first
term. Uh number two, and so obviously
all that's bullish. Number one is
obviously bullish. Uh number two, uh
incremental dysfunction in the Treasury
market will force the Fed to expand its
balance sheet in support of its
financial stability mandate. Uh so,
that's bearish until it gets bullish.
Uh number three, incremental dysfunction
in the Treasury market will force
regulators to accelerate financial
oppression as well. Uh and any you know,
we consider any financial oppression
anything that forces banks uh into the
Treasury market and or forces investors
broadly into the Treasury market, for
example, removing uh the supplemental
supplementary leverage ratio for
Treasury securities, which we expect to
occur at some point this year. Uh and
then number four, uh the Fed may be
forced to hike rates during future
recessions to attract capital to the
Treasury market. Let me say that again.
The Fed may be forced to hike rates
during future recessions to attract
capital to the Treasury market
like emerging market economy central
bankers have to when they're in
recession. Uh this is a big deal. Uh I'm
not smart enough to know if we're
starting this process today,
but I do think we need to
ask and answer these questions. And then
like I said, we're going to do that uh
for you today uh in our fundamental
section before we wrap up. So, uh in
terms of KISS, uh no pivots today.
KISS is uh currently at 70% cash, uh 0%
stocks, 30% gold, and 0% uh Bitcoin.
Let's uh keep moving here. So, the
following content is intended for use by
retail investors and should not be
reconciled with our fundamental research
views or anything we discuss beyond
slide 11 of this presentation. Uh if you
guessed our simple and systematic KISS
portfolio construction process, uh
you've guessed right. Uh if you know uh
just for those who may be new uh kicking
the tires on KISS, uh let me just give
you one quick stat here or two quick
stats rather. Uh if you can't do 20 plus
percent average annual returns with a
max drawdown of minus 12%, so a Calmar
ratio basically two, that's max average
annual return divided by the max
drawdown, uh this is your hurdle that
you need to beat if you're not going to
participate in our KISS portfolio
construction process as a retail
investor. Um you know, KISS is extremely
uh effective at at what it does as
evidenced by you know, the run the epic
run we've been on uh since we launched
it uh back in January 2023.
Um if you wanted to compare KISS, which
again is risk managed exposure to
stocks, gold, and Bitcoin and that
incorporates volatility targeting and
dynamic position sizing to increase and
and decrease the exposures over time,
if you wanted to employ KISS, that that
is KISS. Uh but if you wanted to just be
naked long stocks, gold, Bitcoin, 60 30
10 stocks go Bitcoin all the time with
no dynamic position sizing, no vault
targeting, just 100% exposed to those
assets all the time. KISS has a upside
capture ratio to that strategy, that
naked long strategy, of 98%, but a
downside capture ratio of only 49%. So,
you're essentially getting the same
return that you would if you were naked
long these assets all of the time, but
you're cutting out half of the downside
risk. You're cutting out half the risk.
And so, that's exactly what
institutional investors get paid to do.
We get paid to help investors
participate in bull markets while risk
managing and chopping off the left tail
of the distribution heading into bear
markets just as we've done for you here
in 2025, much like we did to you for you
in December of 2021 heading into that
bear market. So, we have a little
experience We have a lot of experience.
I've never been on the wrong side of a

can say.

Uh in terms of our fundamental research
summary, so uh for those who are new,
these themes are organized in
chronological order of when we introduce
them to you. Uh so we'll start with our
sticky inflation theme, we introduced
that in January of 2022 with the time
horizon of 1 year plus from today. US
inflation is unlikely to durably return
to the Fed's stated 2% and de facto 2.5%
targets without an actual recession, an
NBER-style recession. Since last summer,
our GRID model has been forecasting an
uptrend in inflation throughout 2025.
Although both the Fed and investor
consensus have recently capitulated into
our view, we still anticipate upside
surprises over the medium term. We do
not believe the net impact of easy
comps, tariffs, and restricting
immigration is fully reflected in
consensus estimates. In terms of our
resilient US economy theme, we
introduced that in September of 2022
with a time horizon of 1 year plus from
today. The US economy remains resilient
due to historically strong private
sector balance sheets, dovish monetary
policy, labor hoarding, and labor
hoarding. Leading indicators currently
signal a low probability of an actual
recession over the medium term. Again,
when we say actual, we're talking
NBER-style recession. Technical just
means, you know, consecutive quarters of
negative GDP growth. Uh since last
summer, our US GRID model has been
forecasting a down-trending growth
throughout 2025. Although both the Fed
and investor consensus have recently
capitulated into our view, we still
anticipate uh downside surprises over
the medium term. We do not believe the
net impact of tough comps, historically
elevated policy uncertainty, tariffs
restricting immigration, and DOJ budget
cuts is fully reflected in consensus
estimates. It's not.
A technical recession is likely as
President Trump kitchen sinks the
economy per the view we authored last
fall.
J Powell's soft landing thing, we
introduced that in November of 2023 with
a time horizon through May of 2026 when
Jay Powell is done at the Fed.
The Fed has a modestly dovish reaction
function that is geared towards
engineering a soft landing in the US
economy.
We expect the Fed to end the Treasury
portion of balance sheet runoff in the
first half of 2025. The Fed is
implementing its typical fourth turning
playbook, which is to support fiscal
dominance by artificially engineering
demand for US Treasuries amid
above-target inflation and explosive
growth in federal debt. Fiscal dominance
demands substantial monetary debasement
and financial repression over the long
term. And this is, you know, this is
They need to support fiscal dominance by
artificially engineering demand for US
Treasuries amid above-target inflation
and explosive growth in federal debt.
Normally, that is cutting interest rate
cutting interest rates and doing QE.
In a world where, you know, the the rest
in a in a regime where the rest of the
world is very concerned about the US's
twin deficit dynamics,
then it may actually include hiking
rates and doing QE. Right? Think about
that. You might have to hike rates to
attract capital and do QE to maintain
the the functioning the smooth
functioning of the market. I mean, this
is this is I I quite frankly,
you heard it here first. At some point
in the next 5 years, that may be an
outcome. The Fed has to hike rates in
recession and do QE while while doing
QE. It it sounds crazy to say today, but
again, this is this is based on our

is likely headed if we stay on this

accelerating the stepping on the gas in

turning, polycrisis that we've been

currently in.
Triple S theme, uh we introduced that in
uh November 2024 uh with a time horizon
into and through the fourth quarter of
2025.
The economy and asset markets may
struggle amid uncertainty regarding the
size, sequence, scope, and scope of
potentially dramatic changes to fiscal,
regulatory, and trade policy, especially
um it's from asymmetric starting points
in the growth and positioning cycles.
First, the size of President Trump's tax
cut proposals may get haircut by deficit
hawks in Congress. Moreover, the
sequence of policy implementation may
cause problems due to the negative
supply shocks from tariffs and
restricting immigration and the negative
demand shock from DOE occurring before
the positive supply shocks from tax cuts
and deregulation. Worse, the economic
impact of the positive and negative
positive and negative fiscal,
regulatory, and trade policy shocks is
likely to be negative on a net basis.
For the Q1 QRA and Treasury Secretary
Scott Bessent, the scope of Treasury net
financing policy is unlikely to evolve
in a hawkish manner for at least the
next several quarters. That outcome is
positive for US liquidity. The debt
limit breach is also positive for US
liquidity for the following two reasons.
Number one, it is perpetuating a
significant reduction in the Treasury
General Account balance. Number two, it
reduces net financing to zero, which
frees up investor balance sheet capacity
to capitalize risk assets. Moreover, the
scope of tariff policy, specifically
reciprocal tariffs, lowers the risk of a
too strong US dollar that reduces global
liquidity because the US and its trading
partners may opt to negotiate lower
trade barriers than offset fresh tariffs
via currency devaluation. Lastly, the
potential beginning of the end of the
US's exorbitant privilege may catalyze a
monetary inflation if the Fed is forced
to backstop the Treasury market, and
they may very well may very well may
they may very well will be forced if we
continue to see the kind of dysfunction
that we saw

indicates rotational flows have been

medium-term positioning indicators
indicate retail traders are underweight
stocks and active managers are
underweight stocks. Speculators are

commodities. Our positioning model
indicates moderate risk of a rally in

medium-term. In terms of our volatility

horsemen of the market risk the high
beta low beta ratio is bearish. The
cyclicals defenses ratio is bearish. The
value to growth ratio is bullish and the
small cap mega cap ratio is bearish.
Risk assets are mixed stocks are bearish
cryptos are bearish commodities are
mixed defensive assets are mixed

index is neutral. The CVX currency ball

tend to get resolved in a three to 12
plus month time horizon. Our global
macro risk matrix indicates Uh is the
current market regime. Deflation is a
risk off regime in which investors are
generally rewarded for reducing risk
because policymakers are unlikely to
adequately support real economic growth
that is perceived to be decelerating or
persistently lower than expectations.
The key portfolio construction
considerations in deflation are
defensive assets tend to outperform risk
assets. Low beta tends to outperform
high beta from a factor standpoint.
Defensive tends to outperform cyclicals
from a sector standpoint. Growth tends
to outperform value. Large caps tend to
outperform small and mid caps. The US
tends to outperform international.
Developed markets tend to outperform
emerging markets. Treasuries tend to
outperform spread products in the fixed
income market. Long rates tend to
outperform the belly which tend to
outperform short rates in the Treasury
curve. Investment grade tends to
outperform high yield in the credit
market. Ag commodities tend to
outperform energy commodities which tend
to outperform industrial commodities.
And then finally gold tends to
outperform the US dollar which tends to
outperform foreign currency. So uh
